title: Bircher muesli
subtitle: Oats soaked in apple juice overnight with grated apple and yoghurt.
excerpt: Jumbo oats soaked overnight in apple juice with lemon, grated apple, and plain yoghurt. Topped with toasted almonds and honey. The Swiss original, simpler and less sweet than most commercial versions.
sourceNotes: Bircher muesli was developed around 1900 by Swiss physician Maximilian Bircher-Benner for patients at his Zurich sanatorium. The original preparation, oats soaked overnight in water and lemon juice mixed with grated apple, condensed milk, and nuts, was a radical departure from the cooked breakfasts of the era. The version that reached Britain, and appears in Swiss hotel breakfasts, uses yoghurt and apple juice in place of the condensed milk and water.

content.text: Bircher muesli and overnight oats look similar but taste different. Apple juice gives Bircher a faint sharpness that milk does not, and the grated apple adds texture as well as flavour. The oats stay slightly more distinct than milk-soaked oats.
Use a tart eating apple such as a Cox or Braeburn. Sweet apples like Gala produce a blander result. The lemon juice keeps the grated apple from browning and adds a sharpness that balances the honey.
A medium bowl and a box grater for the apple. No cooking equipment.

Combine {{jumbo-oats}} of oats, {{apple-juice}} of apple juice, {{plain-yoghurt}} of yoghurt, and {{honey}} of honey in a bowl. Grate the apple directly into the bowl, skin and all. Add the lemon juice and stir well. Cover and refrigerate for at least 6 hours.
Stir the Bircher in the morning. Adjust the consistency with a splash of apple juice if too thick. Spoon into bowls and top with toasted flaked almonds and blueberries if using.

items.fix: The flavour is unchanged. Add more lemon juice next time and stir it in immediately after grating the apple.
items.cause: Not enough lemon juice to prevent oxidation.

items.fix: Loosen with a splash of apple juice or milk and stir.
items.cause: Jumbo oats absorb more liquid than porridge oats.

items.fix: Add a little more honey. A sweeter apple variety also reduces sharpness, though some sharpness is correct.
items.cause: Tart apple variety or too much lemon.

items.fix: Bircher needs at least 6 hours. Jumbo oats take longer to soften than porridge oats. Add more apple juice and leave longer.
items.cause: Too little soaking time or too little liquid.
items.symptom: Oats are still chewy after soaking

content.text: Bircher muesli was first made at a Swiss sanatorium in the late 1800s. It started as a hospital breakfast built on raw oats and raw fruit. Within a few decades the dish had spread to Swiss hotel breakfasts and on through Europe. It reached British hotel buffets before it reached home kitchens. That is why it kept the Swiss name rather than picking up an English one.
